title: Hell on Wheels Netflix show is too violent

enrichment.summary: Kyle reviews the Netflix Western show Hell on Wheels, critiquing it for excessive and gratuitous violence while arguing the real Wild West was far less violent than depicted. He uses historical examples to support his point and reflects on the societal effects of normalizing on-screen violence, while admitting he is still watching the show.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: too gratuitously violent and not historically accurate
was far more law-abiding and civilized than pop culture portrays
hypocritical that violence gets a pass while nudity is censored
programs viewers, especially kids, to normalize and emulate violent behavior
should base outlandish events on real documented stories rather than invented gratuitous ones
enjoys the show's racial/social drama but feels guilty watching the violence

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I was watching the television show 'Wheels' and I was enjoying it.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I don't think I should be proud of watching 'Wheels' because it's so violent.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I was thinking about the scene where the guy strangles the blonde girl to death, and that's my happy show.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The show 'Wheels' is a good example of what's wrong with our society.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The show 'Wheels' reflects what's wrong with the way we look at history, the past, and our own human nature.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The show 'Wheels' shows how we don't take our lives seriously.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: We shouldn't be representing people as being that evil in shows like 'Wheels'.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The show 'Wheels' wasn't that violent in the sense that people weren't just gratuitously killing each other.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: In 'Wheels', every season somebody has to die or do something completely violent for no reason.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: In 'Wheels', there's a scene where three guys from the railroad town go out to get revenge on the Indians who attacked the rail car.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: In 'Wheels', the army guy kills the Indian guy for no reason after chatting with him.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: In 'Wheels', the Swedish guy strangles the girl to death when they could have just had her go back to Chicago.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The Wild West wasn't as violent as portrayed in 'Wheels'; people weren't just meticulously killing each other.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Caribou, one of the biggest mining towns in Colorado, had only one murder in 50 years.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: In Caribou, an Irish guy got in a fight with a black guy over food and killed him, but they acquitted the white guy.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Wild Bill Hickok, the most famous gunslinger, only killed six people in his whole life.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The guy who killed Wild Bill Hickok was a random dude who walked into his card game and shot him.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The Wild West had a lot of law and order, and people were always trying to maintain their order in better ways.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: In the Oregon Trail, a woman wrote a book about her experiences, including an incident where a guy was killed and nobody knew who did it.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: In the Oregon Trail, they hung the guy they suspected of killing the wagon guy but took care of his family for the rest of the way.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The Mormons in 'Wheels' are portrayed as shooting and killing a cop for no reason.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: In the Oregon Trail book, the Mormons had a bad reputation because they were accused of ambushing wagon trains and kidnapping women.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Historical TV shows should focus on the most outrageous but true stories rather than inventing things that don't make sense.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The most outrageous true stories from history are a lot more tame than the stuff they put in 'Wheels'.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The show 'Wheels' is too violent and gives people nightmares.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The show 'Wheels' is rated PG-13 despite its violence because it has no nudity.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Marco Polo, another Netflix original series, has a lot of nudity but not much actual violence.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The show 'Wheels' is programming people to think that people in the past were gratuitously violent and vengeful.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: People should try to cut down on consuming violent content like 'Wheels' even if they enjoy it.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm addicted to 'Wheels' and will keep watching it, but I think we should cut down on the violent behavior portrayed in it.

transcript.text: Here's a movie review. I haven't done one of these for a while, but I wanted to do a movie review, TV review, of the television show Hell on Wheels, because I was mentioning the other day that I was watching it and I was enjoying it, and I was laying in bed last night, like, I felt kind of embarrassed I said that, because it's, I don't think it's a, it's not a show that I should be proud of watching, because it's so violent.

I think it's gratuitously violent. I kept, I was looking, thinking about the scene where the guy strangles the blonde girl to death, and, you know, and that's like what I choose to watch as my happy show.
I think that show is a good example of what's wrong with our society. We have a, and what's wrong with our, the way that we look at history and look at the past, and look at our own human nature.

And how we, it shows how we don't take our lives seriously. We shouldn't be representing people as being that evil. It wasn't that violent. I mean, people, if you think about, talk about the Wild West, people say, oh yeah, it was violent.

But it wasn't violent in that people were just gratuitously killing people, each other. In that show, everybody has to die, like, every season somebody has to die or somebody has to do something completely violent, like, for no reason.
For example, there's this one scene where they get three guys from the town, the railroad town, to go out and get revenge on the Indians that attacked the rail, the rail car.
So they have an Indian in there who knows where they are, because it's his family, and then a white guy and a black guy go out, and then the army.
So they go out and they kill the Indians, and then the Indian guy and the army guy chat for a little bit after, after it all, and the Indian guy walks off and everything's good.
And then in the next scene, that army guy shows up inside of the preacher man's tent, and he says, hey, where's that Indian guy?
And all of a sudden, the preacher man's like, hey, you don't want to kill my son.
And he's like, he's like, I just want to talk to him.
And he turns around, and then he just slices his head off.
The preacher man slices the head off the soldier for no reason.
And then that's that story.
And then the girl, the blonde girl, they keep telling her, they try to get her to move back to Chicago.
And then they, but she doesn't want to, and she's afraid, oh, they might try to kill her.
And he was thinking, he tried to get the black guy to kill her, but he didn't want to.
So then the Swedish guy strangles her to death.
And you film that, when they could have just had her go back.
If they wanted to get the character out, they didn't have to get her killed.
When the preacher man kills all those people before he dies.
I don't want to give all the examples, but the Wild West wasn't that violent.
People were just gratuitously killing people, each other.
For example, in the mining town, Caribou, it was one of the biggest mining towns in Colorado.
It had, some people say, up to 10,000 people.
It had like six saloons.
And there was only one murder the whole time, and it existed for like 50 years.
An Irish guy got in a fight with a black guy.
The only black guy who lived up there over food and killed the black guy, and then they acquitted the white guy.
But usually people would be acquitted.
If somebody would kill somebody, then they would go into like a court of locals, of like six locals, to investigate it.
And then, but they would always acquit the person.
But that didn't really happen, like all the time.
Like it shows, Wild Bill Hickok, the guy who's most famous, the most famous gunslinger.
He only killed six people in his whole life.
And he was kind of like a lawman, you know.
So, and the guy who killed him was just a crazy random dude who walked into his card game while his back was turned to the wall.
And just shot him in the head because he wanted to.
And that guy got acquitted.
Before they, you know, there was a public outcry and then they eventually hung him.
But, but, I mean, the Wild West was, the Wild West was something that was, there was a lot of law and order and people were always trying to maintain their law and order.
And there were better ways to portray how people would, you know, watch after themselves, I guess, the security.
There's one story, like in the Oregon Trail, there was a woman who wrote a book about the Oregon Trail, her experiences.
And there was one incident where a guy was killed.
One of the wagon guys was killed and nobody knew who killed him.
He just was dead one morning.
And after a few weeks or whatever, they started seeing his belongings in this other guy's wagon train.
And so they decided that he, that he did it.
And so they all hung him and they, but they made sure to take care of his family for the rest of the way because he had, you know, a wife and kids.
So, um, there are a lot of, uh, um, a lot of, uh, ways thinking they could have portrayed those people as being a lot more civilized.
They showed the, uh, the Mormons, uh, in this last episode I saw.
And, uh, they walk up to talk to the Mormon and then they just shoot the cop and kill him.
Um, um, I remember in the Oregon Trail book, they were talking about the Mormons and she said that they were, they were afraid of them.
They had bad reputation from the wagon train because they were known to ambush the wagon train, kind of like Indians and kidnap women as wives.
Um, but, uh, and yeah, so they could have shown something like that, but just, uh, people being stupid, you know, and then him, him giving up his son to be hung.
I've never heard anything like that.
Um, yeah, every episode having, uh, something completely outlandish happening every single episode.
Um, I think we should cut that out on the historical TV shows and we should bring them, uh, stress more.

The most outlandish crazy story that ever actually happened that somebody read about and then put that in the story, even if it doesn't have to be in the same context, but not to invent things that don't make sense.
You know, because the most outlandish stuff, craziest, wackiest, wildest stories that ever actually really existed are a lot more tame than the stuff they put in that show.
Um, um, so yeah, I think it's, it's too violent, man.
Um, it's too bad that you have to, because I do like watching it for all the, the drama between the races and the, and the showing how interesting of a situation that is for everybody.
But, um, but, um, but to have to have all of these just violent scenes, you know, the guy getting his head chopped off, the girl getting strangled, the son having to stab his father to death.
Um, um, those two brothers gutting the, um, killing the, uh, butcher, you know, the butcher wanting to kill them because it's just a revenge.
And I mean, there was a lot of other drama that you could show that would be more entertaining.
That wouldn't like give somebody nightmares as they're lying in bed.
Like, Oh my God, I can't believe I'm watching that.
Like, what the fuck?
And the, this fucked up thing is that, that show, um, with all of the violence it has, it's, it's, it's one of the like G rated shows, PG 13, because there's no, there's no, uh, nudity at all.
And I was watching Marco Polo, which is another Netflix original super miniseries.
And every single episode, there were like 50 completely naked girls.
And I don't think there was any of actual violence.
But in this show, like half the characters are prostitutes and you know, they're having sex and there's like no nipple yet at the same time, there's, uh, there's slicing heads off.

And so we're telling the kids it's okay to be violent and just show violence and talk about violence and, and try to tell them that, you know, give them the idea that back then people were just gratuitously violent and vengeful like that.
And that's, that's how, that's how people spent their time.
People are going to grow up and become like that.
Obviously that's, it's like programming people to be like that.
We should program people to be, uh, we should, they should have cut out all that stuff and had, um, yeah.
Um, more of the politics and stuff.
So, but, um, yeah, but I mean, I'm addicted to it.
We're watching it, uh, last night for a bunch of episodes and, uh, I think I'll keep watching it.
But I mean, yeah, I mean, maybe, you know, they, the people who make it, they know what, what's entertaining.
Um, so I guess people should try to, uh, it's kind of like junk food, you know, junk entertainment.
It's delicious and just scrumptious, I guess, but it's not healthy.
So we should, we have to actually try to forcefully cut it down.
Not, not the people who make it because they want to make money, but the people who actually consume it need to say, okay, I like the show, but I reject.
I think we should cut down that just the, uh, all of the violent bad behavior that they were doing.
So that's what I wanted to say.
Okay, peace out.
